section.title: Final Warnings and Benediction
section.sectionRationale: The letter closes with warning and grace held together. Paul comes a third time, and every matter will be settled by two or three witnesses; he will not spare the unrepentant, for Christ who was crucified in weakness now speaks powerfully through him. He turns the test back on the church, Examine yourselves to see whether you are in the faith, then prays for their restoration, and ends with the fullest trinitarian blessing in his letters: the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, the love of God, and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit.
section.thesis: Christ crucified in weakness yet living by God's power is the pattern for both apostolic authority and the believer's life, so the church must examine itself, aim for restoration, and live in the grace of the triune God.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the final warning established by witnesses and grounded in the crucified yet living Christ; the call to self-examination and the prayer for restoration; and the closing appeal with the trinitarian benediction.

units.label: Established by witnesses
units.note: crucified in weakness, living by God's power

verses.text: This is the third time I am coming to you. “Every matter must be established by the testimony of two or three witnesses.”
I already warned you the second time I was with you. So now in my absence I warn those who sinned earlier and everyone else: If I return, I will not spare anyone,
since you are demanding proof that Christ is speaking through me. He is not weak in dealing with you but is powerful among you.
For He was indeed crucified in weakness, yet He lives by God's power. For we are also weak in Him, yet by God's power we will live with Him concerning you.

units.label: Examine yourselves
units.note: test whether you are in the faith

verses.text: {{dokimazo|Examine}} yourselves to see whether you are in the faith; test yourselves. Do you not realize that Jesus Christ is in you, unless you fail the test?
And I hope you will realize that we have not failed the test.
Now we pray to God that you will not do anything wrong, not that we will appear to have stood the test, but that you will do what is right, even if we appear to have failed.
For we cannot do anything against the truth, but only for the truth.
In fact, we rejoice when we are weak but you are strong, and our prayer is for your {{katartisis|perfection}}.
This is why I write these things while absent, so that when I am present I will not need to be severe in my use of the authority that the Lord gave me for building you up, not for tearing you down.

units.label: The grace of the triune God
units.note: aim for restoration, live in peace

verses.text: Finally, brothers, rejoice! Aim for perfect harmony, encourage one another, be of one mind, live in peace. And the God of love and peace will be with you.
Greet one another with a holy kiss.
All the saints send you greetings.
The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, and the love of God, and the {{koinonia|fellowship}} of the Holy Spirit be with all of you.

dokimazo.gloss: To examine, to test, to prove by trial as metal is assayed. Paul turns the Corinthians' demand for proof of his apostleship back upon themselves: they are to test whether they are in the faith. The related word for passing or failing the test runs through the passage. The aim is not morbid doubt but honest self-scrutiny that leads to assurance that Jesus Christ is in the believer.

katartisis.gloss: Restoration, mending, being put into full order, as a broken bone is set or a torn net is repaired. Paul's prayer is for the Corinthians' restoration, their being brought to completeness and maturity. The related verb appears again in the appeal to aim for perfect harmony. The word joins the mending of what is broken with the reaching of what is intended, a fitting hope for a divided church.

koinonia.gloss: Fellowship, sharing, communion, participation together in something held in common. In the closing blessing the fellowship of the Holy Spirit is the shared life the Spirit creates among believers and between them and God. It is not mere friendliness but genuine partaking in the life of God, binding the church into one body and drawing it into the love of the Father and the grace of the Son.

basic.context: Paul draws the letter to a close with a firm warning and a tender blessing. Coming a third time, he says every charge will be settled by two or three witnesses, and he will not spare the unrepentant, for the Christ who speaks through him is not weak but powerful. He then turns the tables: instead of testing him, the Corinthians should examine themselves to see whether they are truly in the faith, with Christ in them. He prays for their restoration and ends with the great threefold blessing of grace, love, and fellowship.
basic.hermeneutics: Hold the warning and the grace together; the letter ends with both. The key move is in verse 4, where Christ crucified in weakness yet living by God's power becomes the pattern for Paul's ministry and for the church. Read Examine yourselves not as a recipe for endless anxiety but as a call to honest self-testing that ends in assurance. The trinitarian benediction gathers the whole letter into the life of the triune God.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul calls them to dokimazo, to test and prove themselves, the very examination they had demanded of him. His prayer is for their katartisis, their restoration and mending into full order. And the blessing ends with the koinonia of the Holy Spirit, the shared communion the Spirit creates with God and with one another.
basic.typology: The rule of two or three witnesses is drawn straight from the law of Moses, now applied in the church. Christ crucified in weakness yet raised in power fulfills the pattern glimpsed throughout Scripture, that God brings life through apparent defeat. The prayer for restoration echoes the prophets' longing for a mended people, fulfilled as Christ puts His body together in maturity.
basic.christ: Christ is the center of the closing. He was crucified in weakness yet lives by God's power, and this is both the ground of Paul's authority and the shape of the believer's life. The test of true faith is whether Jesus Christ is in you. And the benediction begins with the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, from whom every other blessing flows, revealing the Son as one with the Father and the Spirit.
basic.god: The passage ends on the triune God: the grace of the Son, the love of the Father, and the fellowship of the Spirit. God is the God of love and peace who dwells with a reconciled people. He is the source of the power by which Christ lives and by which Paul serves, and He gives authority for building up rather than tearing down. The whole letter closes inside the life of this God.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 19 gives the rule of two or three witnesses. Matthew 28 shows the triune name in baptism. 1 Corinthians 11 uses the same call to self-examination before the Lord's table.
basic.oneStory: The Bible moves from a world fractured by sin to a people restored and dwelling with God. Here the letter ends by pressing the church to examine itself, aim for restoration, and live in peace, all within the grace of Father, Son, and Spirit. Christ crucified in weakness and living in power is the hinge, and the triune blessing points to the communion with God that is the goal of the whole story.

context.literary: The chapter gathers the letter's great themes for a final time: weakness and power, authority and building up, warning and love. Verse 4 restates in miniature the paradox of chapter 12, now applied to Christ Himself and then to Paul. The mood turns from warning to blessing across verses 11 to 14, ending with the most complete trinitarian benediction in Paul, a fitting close to a letter so concerned with the sufficiency of God's grace.
context.historical: Paul had visited twice, the second a painful visit, and this third coming looms over the whole letter. The demand for proof that Christ spoke through him reflects the Corinthians' fascination with power and the false apostles' challenge to his authority. The holy kiss was the customary greeting of the early church, and the mention of all the saints situates Corinth within the wider fellowship of believers.
context.canonical: 2 Corinthians 13:14 is one of the New Testament's clearest windows into the triune life of God, naming Son, Father, and Spirit together in a single blessing, and it has been used in Christian worship ever since. The call to examine yourselves stands alongside 1 Corinthians 11 as a foundation for the church's practice of self-examination, and the two or three witnesses rule shows the enduring authority of the law's principles.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To warn the unrepentant plainly before his arrival, to redirect the Corinthians from testing Paul to testing themselves, and to leave them with a call to restoration and peace under the grace of the triune God, so that his authority may build them up rather than tear them down.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specifics of the third visit and the witnesses belong to Paul's dealings with Corinth. The abiding, prescriptive truths are the call to self-examination, the pattern of Christ crucified in weakness and living in power, and the trinitarian grace that shapes Christian life and worship.

debates.question: Does Examine yourselves teach that assurance of salvation is uncertain or works-based?

views.label: A call to honest self-examination that yields assurance
views.case: Paul expects them to realize that Jesus Christ is in them; the test looks for the presence of Christ by faith, not a tally of works, and aims at settled assurance rather than anxiety.

views.label: A warning that some may be self-deceived
views.case: The clause unless you fail the test allows that some professions are false, so the examination guards against presuming on a faith that is not real.

debates.bearing: Both guard the same truth: genuine faith shows itself in Christ dwelling within, and self-examination is a means of grace leading to assurance, not a return to works-righteousness.

debates.question: What does the trinitarian benediction reveal about God?

views.label: A settled confession of the triune God
views.case: The blessing names the Son, the Father, and the Spirit together as the one source of grace, love, and fellowship, an early and clear witness to the Trinity woven into worship.

views.label: A rich blessing not yet a formal doctrine
views.case: Some note that Paul is praying a blessing, not writing a creed, so the developed doctrine of the Trinity is later, though its raw material is plainly here.

debates.bearing: The verse is not a technical formula, yet it names the three persons in one breath as the one God who blesses, and the church rightly reads it as a genuine and treasured witness to the triune God.

study.originalLanguages: Paul tells the Corinthians to dokimazo, to test and prove themselves, the very scrutiny they had aimed at him, asking whether they are in the faith and whether Jesus Christ is in them. His longing for them is katartisis, restoration, the mending of what is broken and its being set into full order, the same idea he presses when he urges them to aim for perfect harmony. And the letter closes on the koinonia of the Holy Spirit, the shared communion the Spirit creates, binding believers into the love of the Father and the grace of the Son, so that the last word of the letter is fellowship with the triune God.

typology.fulfillment: The law of Moses applied within the church, showing the enduring justice of God's word now serving the peace and order of Christ's people.

typology.type: Crucified in weakness, living by God's power
typology.fulfillment: Christ fulfills the pattern woven through Scripture that God brings life through apparent defeat, and this becomes the shape of the believer's own life in Him.

study.christ: Christ dominates the close of the letter. He was crucified in weakness yet lives by God's power, and this paradox grounds both Paul's authority and the church's hope. He speaks through His apostle, He dwells within His people as the mark of true faith, and His grace heads the final blessing. The whole letter, so full of weakness and power, resolves in the crucified and risen Lord whose grace is named first among the gifts of the triune God, showing Him one with the Father and the Spirit.
study.god: The passage ends on the fullness of God: the love of the Father, the grace of the Son, and the fellowship of the Spirit. God is the God of love and peace who promises to be with a people who aim for restoration and live in harmony. He is the source of the power by which Christ lives and Paul serves, and He gives His servants authority for building up. The letter closes by drawing the church into communion with this triune God, which is the very goal of salvation.

crossReferences.note: Every matter established by two or three witnesses, the rule Paul applies to his coming judgment at Corinth.
The same call to examine oneself, there before the Lord's Supper, guarding the church's integrity.
Baptism in the name of the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit, the triune name echoed in the benediction.
The fellowship of the Spirit and the call to be of one mind, paralleling Paul's closing appeal for harmony.

study.oneStory: Scripture runs from a world broken by sin toward a people restored and dwelling with God in peace. The letter ends by urging that restoration: examine yourselves, be mended, live in harmony, all within the grace of Father, Son, and Spirit. Christ crucified in weakness yet living in power is the turning point, and the closing blessing opens onto the communion with the triune God that is the destination of the whole biblical story.

quiz.q: On what basis does Paul say every matter will be settled when he comes?

options.text: By the testimony of two or three witnesses, drawn from the law.
options.note: Verse 1 cites the rule of witnesses from Deuteronomy.

options.text: By his own apostolic word alone, without any witnesses.
options.note: Paul deliberately invokes the standard of two or three witnesses, not his bare word.

options.text: By a vote of the whole congregation.
options.note: The passage appeals to witnesses, not to a congregational vote.

options.text: By casting lots to discover the guilty.
options.note: There is no casting of lots here; the standard is confirmed testimony.

quiz.q: What does Christ's being crucified in weakness yet living by God's power establish?

options.text: The pattern for both Paul's ministry and the believer's life, weak in Him yet living by God's power.
options.note: Verse 4 applies the pattern to us who are weak in Him.

options.text: That Christ was only appearing to be weak on the cross.
options.note: His weakness was real, yet He truly lives by God's power.

options.text: That power and weakness have nothing to do with each other.
options.note: The point is precisely that God's power works through weakness.

options.text: That believers should avoid all weakness at any cost.
options.note: Paul embraces weakness as the place where God's power is shown.

quiz.q: What does Paul mean by Examine yourselves to see whether you are in the faith?

options.text: Test honestly whether Jesus Christ is truly in you, leading to assurance.
options.note: Verse 5 points to the presence of Christ as the mark of genuine faith.

options.text: Count up your good works to earn a passing grade with God.
options.note: The test looks for Christ within by faith, not a tally of works.

options.text: Live in constant doubt about whether you are saved.
options.note: The aim is settled assurance, not perpetual anxiety.

options.text: Judge everyone else's faith before your own.
options.note: Paul turns the testing inward, onto yourselves.

quiz.q: What is the shape of the closing benediction?

options.text: The grace of Christ, the love of God, and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit.
options.note: Verse 14 gives the fullest trinitarian blessing in Paul.

options.text: The grace of Christ alone, without the Father or the Spirit.
options.note: The blessing names all three persons together, not Christ only.

options.text: A blessing directed only to the apostles among them.
options.note: It is spoken over all of you, the whole church.

options.text: A wish for material prosperity and comfort.
options.note: The blessing is grace, love, and fellowship with the triune God, not prosperity.

questions: Paul told the Corinthians to test themselves rather than test him. When did you last honestly examine whether you are in the faith, and what did you find?
The mark of true faith is that Jesus Christ is in you. What evidences of Christ's presence and work can you actually see in your life?
Christ was crucified in weakness yet lives by God's power. How does that pattern reshape the way you view your own weakness and failure?
Paul prayed for their restoration and urged them to live in peace. Is there a broken relationship in the church or your life that God may be calling you to help mend?
The letter ends with the grace of the Son, the love of the Father, and the fellowship of the Spirit. How might dwelling on the triune God who blesses you change your worship this week?

translationNotes.note: The quotation Every matter must be established by the testimony of two or three witnesses is drawn from Deuteronomy 19:15; the BSB keeps it in quotation marks, retained here with curly quotes.

Do you not realize that Jesus Christ is in you, unless you fail the test is rendered without dashes; the clause unless you fail the test qualifies the whole, allowing that a false profession would not stand.

The BSB footnote observes that texts vary in numbering the last verses of the chapter, and that the Byzantine text ends with Amen; the BSB numbering of verses 1 to 14 is followed here, with 13:14 as the final verse.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 2 Corinthians 13:1-14. Em dashes removed and replaced with commas or clean breaks, notably in 13:5 and 13:7. Curly quotes retained around the quotation in 13:1. Footnote markers stripped. BSB numbering of verses 1 to 14 kept, with 13:14 as the last verse. Sermon set to none; no sermon URL verified this session.
